title: Tony Janiro

infobox.value: (born **Anthony Gianiro**; February 2, 1926 – February 21, 1985) was an American [middleweight](/wiki/Middleweight "Middleweight") [boxer](/wiki/Boxing "Boxing") from [Youngstown](/wiki/Youngstown,_Ohio "Youngstown, Ohio"), [Ohio](/wiki/Ohio "Ohio"). Janiro never won a championship, although he faced many of the top fighters of his era. Despite his reputation as a playboy who avoided training, Janiro compiled a record of 83 wins (26 KOs), 11 losses, and two draws.[[1]](#cite_note-vindy-02-22-85-1)
"Graziano Knocks Out Janiro in 10th in Detroit". *The New York Times*. September 20, 1951. p. 40.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

American boxer (1926–1985)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/2/2b/TJaniro.jpg)](/wiki/File:TJaniro.jpg)

Tony Janiro

**Tony Janiro** (born **Anthony Gianiro**; February 2, 1926 – February 21, 1985) was an American [middleweight](/wiki/Middleweight "Middleweight") [boxer](/wiki/Boxing "Boxing") from [Youngstown](/wiki/Youngstown,_Ohio "Youngstown, Ohio"), [Ohio](/wiki/Ohio "Ohio"). Janiro never won a championship, although he faced many of the top fighters of his era. Despite his reputation as a playboy who avoided training, Janiro compiled a record of 83 wins (26 KOs), 11 losses, and two draws.[[1]](#cite_note-vindy-02-22-85-1)

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tony_Janiro&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

Janiro was born in [Springdale, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Springdale,_Pennsylvania "Springdale, Pennsylvania"). His family relocated to [Youngstown, Ohio](/wiki/Youngstown,_Ohio "Youngstown, Ohio"), when he was four years old.[[1]](#cite_note-vindy-02-22-85-1) He left Youngstown for [New York](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") at the age of 16 to pursue a career in boxing.[[2]](#cite_note-vindy-02-22-85b-2)

## Boxing career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tony_Janiro&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Boxing career")]

Janiro received advice and assistance from fellow Youngstown native Lenny "Boom Boom" Mancini (father of [Ray Mancini](/wiki/Ray_Mancini "Ray Mancini")), who introduced Janiro to his manager, Frankie Jacobs, and boxing trainer [Ray Arcel](/wiki/Ray_Arcel "Ray Arcel").[[2]](#cite_note-vindy-02-22-85b-2) In the 1940s, Janiro was ranked among the top 10 middleweights and fought such Hall of Famers as [Rocky Graziano](/wiki/Rocky_Graziano "Rocky Graziano"), [Jake LaMotta](/wiki/Jake_LaMotta "Jake LaMotta"), and [Kid Gavilan](/wiki/Kid_Gavil%C3%A1n "Kid Gavilán").[[1]](#cite_note-vindy-02-22-85-1) During one bout at [Madison Square Garden](/wiki/Madison_Square_Garden "Madison Square Garden"), the young boxer was introduced to ringside fan [Harry S. Truman](/wiki/Harry_S._Truman "Harry S. Truman"), then President of the United States.[[2]](#cite_note-vindy-02-22-85b-2) Janiro had one draw with Graziano, who knocked him out in 1951.[[3]](#cite_note-nyt-09-20-51-3) He fought from 1943 to 1952.

## Post-boxing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tony_Janiro&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Post-boxing")]

After his retirement, he worked as a bartender at the Neutral Corner, a bar located near [Stillman's Gym](/wiki/Lou_Stillman "Lou Stillman") that was frequented by boxing managers and trainers.[[1]](#cite_note-vindy-02-22-85-1) (The bar is often referred to in journalist [A.J. Liebling](/wiki/A.J._Liebling "A.J. Liebling")'s boxing articles.) Several years before his death, Janiro returned to Youngstown, where he was employed at the [Mahoning County](/wiki/Mahoning_County "Mahoning County") Courthouse.[[1]](#cite_note-vindy-02-22-85-1) In 1984, he was inducted into the Youngstown Curbstone Coaches Hall of Fame, and was honored at a testimonial banquet held in Boardman, Ohio.[[1]](#cite_note-vindy-02-22-85-1) Speakers at the event included former boxing champions [Willie Pep](/wiki/Willie_Pep "Willie Pep"), Jake LaMotta, [Beau Jack](/wiki/Beau_Jack "Beau Jack"), and [Carmen Basilio](/wiki/Carmen_Basilio "Carmen Basilio").[[2]](#cite_note-vindy-02-22-85b-2)

Janiro was portrayed by Kevin Mahon in [Martin Scorsese](/wiki/Martin_Scorsese "Martin Scorsese")'s biopic of [Jake LaMotta](/wiki/Jake_LaMotta "Jake LaMotta"), *[Raging Bull](/wiki/Raging_Bull "Raging Bull")* (1980). In the film, a comment that Janiro looks handsome by LaMotta's wife, [Vicky](/wiki/Vicky_LaMotta "Vicky LaMotta"), provokes a brutal beating in their bout by LaMotta out of jealous rage.

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tony_Janiro&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Personal life")]

Tony Janiro died of kidney failure after suffering a heart attack in his home in the spring of 1985.[[1]](#cite_note-vindy-02-22-85-1) He was survived by his sister, Mrs. Amelia Marian; a brother, Frank of Youngstown, and granddaughter Tracy Janiro Zingaro.[[1]](#cite_note-vindy-02-22-85-1) Funeral services for Janiro were held at St. Christine's [Roman Catholic Church](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Church "Roman Catholic Church"), in Youngstown.[[1]](#cite_note-vindy-02-22-85-1)
